title: The switch Statement 🔌
description: A cleaner way to handle many different exact matches.

slides.title: Too Many if's 🤔
slides.content:
Imagine you are building a menu system where users can press 1 for Play, 2 for Settings, 3 for Credits, 4 for Quit, etc.

Writing `else if (choice == 1) ... else if (choice == 2)` over and over gets messy and slow.

C provides the **`switch`** statement for exactly this scenario.

slides.title: The Switchboard
slides.content:
A `switch` takes a variable and tests it against a list of specific `case` values.

*Important*: Switch cases can ONLY test for direct, exact equality. You cannot do `case > 10`.

quizData.question: What is the equivalent of the `else` fallback block when using a `switch` statement?
quizData.explanation: The `default:` block executes if absolutely none of the `case` values match.

slides.title: The Break Requirement 🛑
slides.content:
Did you notice the **`break;`** statement in every case?

If you forget to write `break;`, C will suffer from **"Fall Through"**. It won't exit the switch; it will just violently continue running the code in the cases directly below it, even if they don't match!

slides.title: Intentional Fall-Through
slides.content: Sometimes "Fall Through" is a feature, not a bug! You can group cases together by leaving out the breaks.

quizData.question: What happens if you completely forget to write `break;` at the end of a matching `case` block in C?

options.text: The program will stop and throw a syntax error.
The program executes the current block, AND continues executing all blocks below it until it hits a break.

quizData.explanation: This is known as 'fall-through'. Once a match is made, C just starts running down the line blindly unless a `break` violently pulls it out.
